title: Why I started taking the longer route home
summary: Explain a deliberate choice to walk a longer route home each day and what that small extra time changed about your week.
topic: Choosing the longer walk home for a reason
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about deciding to take a longer route home from work or school and what that choice gave back to you. Use the present perfect for habits formed over time, a second conditional for a hypothetical, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: There is a shorter way home from my office in Lisbon that I have stopped taking for reasons I struggled to explain at first, because…
If you measured my new route by minutes or steps, you would conclude it makes very little sense, and yet what I have noticed over the past year is…
Although the longer route adds almost fifteen minutes to my walk, what it has given back to me is…

hintVocab.phrase: to take the long way home
hintVocab.meaningVi: đi đường dài hơn về nhà
làm cho đầu óc thoải mái
thư giãn, giải tỏa căng thẳng
đến nơi với tâm thế sẵn sàng
nhận ra khi đi ngang qua
nhịp độ của một thành phố

sampleText:
There is a shorter way home from my office in Lisbon that I have stopped taking for reasons I struggled to explain at first. The direct route follows two busy avenues that connect the centre to my neighbourhood in about eighteen minutes. The longer one, which I have been walking for almost a year now, slips through three quiet streets behind a small church, passes a bakery I never enter, and ends at a square where two old men play chess almost every afternoon. It adds fourteen minutes. If you measured my new route by steps or by minutes, you would conclude it makes very little sense, and at twenty-five I would probably have agreed with you.

What I have noticed, however, is that the longer route works as a kind of transitional space between the version of myself who has to answer emails and the version who comes home to cook dinner and call my mother. The direct route delivers me from one to the other too quickly, as though I were a parcel being moved between two warehouses. I arrive at my door still mentally drafting a reply to a colleague, and the first half of the evening disappears into a tired distraction I do not even notice. The longer route, although it sounds romantic when I describe it this way, is doing something quite practical. It gives me roughly fifteen minutes during which no one expects anything from me, the phone stays in my pocket, and the only decisions I have to make involve whether to cross the street to avoid a puddle.

It has taken me a while to understand why this matters more than it should. We have built a culture in which efficiency is treated as obviously good, where every saved minute is supposed to be invested in something more productive. The longer route quietly refuses that logic. The fifteen minutes I 'lose' each day are minutes during which my work day actually ends, and my evening actually begins. However, I do not want to recommend this to everyone as some kind of universal answer. There are people whose commute is already long enough, or whose city does not offer a route worth taking. What I would say is that the shortest path between two places is not always the shortest path between two versions of yourself, and noticing that distinction may be worth the fourteen extra minutes a day.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical measurement
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If you measured my new route by steps or by minutes, you would conclude it makes very little sense' dùng điều kiện loại 2 — quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề if và 'would + nguyên thể' trong mệnh đề chính — để mô tả một tình huống giả định không có thật.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If you measured my new route by steps or by minutes, you would conclude it makes very little sense' uses the second conditional — past simple in the if-clause and 'would + base verb' in the main — to describe a hypothetical, not a real situation.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for an ongoing habit
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have been walking for almost a year now' và 'I have noticed' dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành (và hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn) để diễn đạt một thói quen bắt đầu từ quá khứ và vẫn đang tiếp tục đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have been walking for almost a year now' and 'I have noticed' use the present perfect (and present perfect continuous) for a habit that started in the past and still continues into the present.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một con đường ngắn hơn từ văn phòng tôi ở Lisbon về nhà mà tôi đã ngừng đi vì những lý do tôi khó giải thích lúc đầu. Tuyến đường trực tiếp đi theo hai đại lộ đông đúc nối trung tâm với khu phố của tôi trong khoảng mười tám phút. Tuyến đường dài hơn, mà tôi đã đi gần một năm nay, len qua ba con phố yên tĩnh phía sau một nhà thờ nhỏ, đi ngang một tiệm bánh tôi chưa bao giờ bước vào, và kết thúc ở một quảng trường nơi hai cụ ông chơi cờ gần như mỗi buổi chiều. Nó cộng thêm mười bốn phút. Nếu bạn đo tuyến đường mới của tôi bằng bước chân hay bằng phút, bạn sẽ kết luận rằng nó rất ít có nghĩa, và ở tuổi hai mươi lăm có lẽ tôi cũng sẽ đồng ý với bạn.

Điều tôi đã nhận ra, tuy nhiên, là tuyến đường dài hơn hoạt động như một loại không gian chuyển tiếp giữa phiên bản của tôi phải trả lời email và phiên bản về nhà nấu ăn và gọi cho mẹ. Tuyến đường trực tiếp đưa tôi từ phiên bản này sang phiên bản kia quá nhanh, như thể tôi là một kiện hàng được chuyển giữa hai kho. Tôi đến cửa nhà mà vẫn đang soạn câu trả lời trong đầu cho một đồng nghiệp, và nửa đầu buổi tối biến mất trong sự sao nhãng mệt mỏi mà tôi thậm chí không nhận ra. Tuyến đường dài hơn, dù nghe lãng mạn khi tôi mô tả theo cách này, đang làm điều gì đó khá thực tế. Nó cho tôi khoảng mười lăm phút trong đó không ai mong đợi điều gì từ tôi, điện thoại nằm trong túi, và những quyết định duy nhất tôi phải đưa ra là có nên qua đường để tránh một vũng nước hay không.

Tôi đã mất một thời gian để hiểu vì sao điều này quan trọng hơn lẽ ra phải vậy. Chúng ta đã xây dựng một nền văn hóa coi sự hiệu quả là hiển nhiên tốt, nơi mỗi phút tiết kiệm được lẽ ra phải được đầu tư vào điều gì đó hiệu quả hơn. Tuyến đường dài hơn lặng lẽ từ chối lô-gic đó. Mười lăm phút tôi 'mất' mỗi ngày là những phút trong đó ngày làm việc của tôi thực sự kết thúc và buổi tối của tôi thực sự bắt đầu. Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn giới thiệu điều này với mọi người như một câu trả lời chung. Có những người mà chuyến đi làm đã đủ dài, hoặc thành phố của họ không có tuyến đường nào đáng đi. Điều tôi sẽ nói là con đường ngắn nhất giữa hai địa điểm không phải lúc nào cũng là con đường ngắn nhất giữa hai phiên bản của chính bạn, và nhận ra sự khác biệt đó có thể đáng giá mười bốn phút thêm mỗi ngày.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect continuous correctly?
options: The longer one, which I have being walking for almost a year now, slips through three quiet streets.
The longer one, which I have been walking for almost a year now, slips through three quiet streets.
The longer one, which I has been walking for almost a year now, slips through three quiet streets.
The longer one, which I have been walk for almost a year now, slips through three quiet streets.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect continuous = 'have/has been + -ing form'. 'I' takes 'have', and the -ing form is 'walking'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If you measure my new route by steps, you would conclude it makes very little sense.
If you measured my new route by steps, you will conclude it makes very little sense.
If you measured my new route by steps, you would conclude it makes very little sense.
If you would measure my new route by steps, you would conclude it makes very little sense.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the main clause. 'Would' does not appear inside the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: The longer one, who I have been walking for almost a year, ends at a square.
The longer one, what I have been walking for almost a year, ends at a square.
The longer one, which I have been walking it for almost a year, ends at a square.
The longer one, which I have been walking for almost a year, ends at a square.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Which' is used for things (a route). 'Who' is for people, 'what' is wrong as a relative pronoun, and the object pronoun 'it' must not be repeated.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Which' for things; do not repeat the object inside the relative clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'as though' + past subjunctive correctly?
options: It delivers me from one to the other too quickly, as though I were a parcel.
It delivers me from one to the other too quickly, as though I am a parcel.
It delivers me from one to the other too quickly, as though I will be a parcel.
It delivers me from one to the other too quickly, as though I being a parcel.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'As though' + past subjunctive ('were' for all persons) describes an unreal comparison.
mcQuestions.hint: 'As though I were' — past subjunctive after 'as though'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' for concession?
options: The longer route, although it sounds romantic, but is doing something quite practical.
The longer route, although it sounds romantic, is doing something quite practical.
Although the longer route, it sounds romantic, is doing something quite practical.
The longer route, although, it sounds romantic, is doing something quite practical.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause' inserted between commas. No 'but' afterwards; no comma immediately after 'although'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'is supposed to be + past participle' correctly?
options: Every saved minute is supposed to invested in something more productive.
Every saved minute is supposed to being invested in something more productive.
Every saved minute is supposed to be invested in something more productive.
Every saved minute is suppose to be invested in something more productive.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be supposed to + base verb', and the base verb here is the passive 'be invested'. The full structure: 'is supposed to be + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whether to + base verb' correctly?
options: The only decisions I have to make involve whether crossing the street to avoid a puddle.
The only decisions I have to make involve whether I cross the street to avoid a puddle or no.
The only decisions I have to make involve whether to crossing the street to avoid a puddle.
The only decisions I have to make involve whether to cross the street to avoid a puddle.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether to + base verb' is the standard structure for an alternative; '-ing' and the awkward 'or no' are wrong.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'treat X as Y' correctly?
options: We have built a culture in which efficiency is treated as obviously good.
We have built a culture in which efficiency is treated like obviously good.
We have built a culture in which efficiency is treated for obviously good.
We have built a culture in which efficiency is treated to obviously good.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Treat X as Y' is the fixed collocation; the passive form 'is treated as' follows the same pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Treat X as Y' → passive 'is treated as Y'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'It has taken me + time period + to + base verb' correctly?
options: It has taken me a while understanding why this matters more than it should.
It has taken me a while to understand why this matters more than it should.
It has took me a while to understand why this matters more than it should.
It is taking me a while to understood why this matters more than it should.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect of 'take' is 'has taken'; the infinitive 'to understand' follows.
mcQuestions.hint: 'It has taken me [time] to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not always' correctly to soften a claim?
options: The shortest path between two places not always is the shortest path between two versions of yourself.
The shortest path between two places is always not the shortest path between two versions of yourself.
The shortest path between two places is not always the shortest path between two versions of yourself.
The shortest path between two places does not always be the shortest path between two versions of yourself.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Be + not always + complement' is the natural English order to express partial frequency. 'Not always be' and 'always not' are unnatural.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'roughly' as a hedge before a number?
options: It gives me rough fifteen minutes during which no one expects anything from me.
It gives me fifteen roughly minutes during which no one expects anything from me.
It gives me roughly of fifteen minutes during which no one expects anything from me.
It gives me roughly fifteen minutes during which no one expects anything from me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Roughly + number + noun' is the standard order, similar to 'about' or 'approximately'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If a longer route 'works' because it inserts unproductive time into your day, is the lesson really about routes, or about how rarely we are allowed to do nothing? How would you defend the longer route to someone who insists every minute should serve a goal?
